blocks.text: Praise the Lord! I will give thanks to the Lord with my whole heart, in the company of the upright, in the congregation.

Ps 111: Hymn to God’s great deeds. Like the immediately following Ps 112 with which it is paired, Ps 111 is a twenty‐two line poem in which each line begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet. Ps 111 praises the righteousness of the , and Ps 112, the righteousness of a person living in accord with divine righteousness.
Great are the works of the Lord, studied by all who delight in them.
Full of honor and majesty is his work, and his righteousness endures forever.
He has gained renown by his wonderful deeds; the Lord is gracious and merciful.
He provides food for those who fear him; he is ever mindful of his covenant.
5 : may refer to the manna and quail that God provided in the wilderness (see Ex 16; Num 11; Ps 105.40).
He has shown his people the power of his works, in giving them the heritage of the nations.
The works of his hands are faithful and just; all his precepts are trustworthy.
7 : The verse is the transition from admiring the divine deeds (v. 2b) to imitating the God who did them (v. 10).
They are established forever and ever, to be performed with faithfulness and uprightness.
He sent redemption to his people; he has commanded his covenant forever. Holy and awesome is his name.
The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom; all those who practice it have a good understanding. His praise endures forever.
10 : (best rendered “revering the ”): see Ps 19.9; Prov 1.7; 9.10; etc.
